elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the superposition of coherent states, emphasizing quantum states with distinct Wigner phase-space features relevant to quantum information applications.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this study, we introduce generalized versions of the compass state, which display enhanced phase-space characteristics compared to the conventional compass state, typically a superposition of four coherent states.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our findings reveal that, unlike sub-Planck structures and phase-space sensitivity of the compass state, these generalized states produce isotropic sub-Planck structures and sensitivity to phase-space displacements.
elements.sent-2411-004: We demonstrate that these desirable phase-space characteristics are maintained in superpositions comprising at least six distinct coherent states.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we show that increasing the number of coherent states in the superposition preserves these characteristics, provided the number remains even.
elements.sent-2411-006: Finally, we examine an optomechanical system capable of generating the proposed quantum states, resulting in optomechanical counterparts with nearly identical phase-space structures, thereby suggesting the feasibility of physically realizing these generalized compass states.

elements.sent-2411-114: External perturbations, such as displacements, can alter a quantum state in phase space, potentially making the displaced quantum state orthogonal to its original form.
elements.sent-2411-115: The phase-space characteristics of a quantum state are intrinsically linked to its sensitivity to such displacements~\cite{toscano_sub-planck_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: This relationship can be examined by evaluating the overlap between a quantum state $\hat{\rho}$ and its slightly displaced counterpart $\hat{\rho _{d = \hat{D}\left(\delta\alpha\right)\hat{\rho}\hat{D ^\dagger\left(\delta\alpha\right)$, where $\delta \alpha = \delta x + \mathrm{i \delta p$~\cite{akhtar_sub-planck_2021 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Here, $\delta x$ and $\delta p$ represent the displacement amounts along the $\text{Re (\alpha)$ and $\text{Im (\alpha)$ directions in phase space, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: This overlap serves as a measure of the quantum state's sensitivity to phase-space displacements.
elements.sent-2411-119: The overlap between a quantum state and its $\delta \alpha$-displaced counterpart is \begin{equation}\label{eq:overlap} O\left(\delta x , \delta p\right) := \mathrm{Tr} \left(\hat{\rho}\hat{\rho}_{d} \right) = \pi \int_{\inf} W_{\hat{\rho}}\left(\alpha\right) W_{\hat{\rho}} \left(\alpha+\delta \alpha \right) \mathrm{d}^2 \alpha.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}\label{eq:overlap} O\left(\delta x , \delta p\right) := Tr \left(\hat{\rho}\hat{\rho}_{d} \right) = \pi \int_{\inf} W_{\hat{\rho}}\left(\alpha\right) W_{\hat{\rho}} \left(\alpha+\delta \alpha \right) d^2 \alpha.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-120: This relationship shows that sensitivity to displacement is proportional to the finer scale available in the phase space for quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-121: That is, states with smaller features exhibit greater sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-122: For instance, compass states possess sub-Planck phase-space characteristics, leading to sensitivity beyond the typical Planck limits~\cite{zurek_sub-planck_2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-123: A slightly displaced $L$-component cat state is denoted as \begin{equation}\hat{D}\left( \delta \alpha\right)\ket{\beta_L}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L}} \sum_{j=0}^{L-1} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}\left(-\beta \delta x \sin{\omega_j} + \beta \delta p \cos{\omega_j} \right)}\ket{\beta \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}\omega_j}+\delta \alpha},\end{equation} where $\omega_j = \nicefrac{2 \pi j {L}$, and the overlap function is \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} O\left(\delta x,\delta p\right) = \left\lvert\braket{\beta_L \mid \hat{D}\left( \delta \alpha\right) \mid \beta_L } \right\rvert^2= \frac{\mathrm{e}^{ - 2\beta^2 - |\delta \alpha|^2}}{L^2}\times \left\lvert \sum_{j,k}^{L-1} \exp{\left[ \beta^2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}(\omega_j-\omega_k)}- \beta \delta \alpha^*\mathrm{e}^{+\mathrm{i}\omega_j } + \beta \delta \alpha\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i}\omega_k} \right] }\right\rvert ^2. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In the limit where $\beta \gg 1$, only the terms with $j=k$ in the summation contribute significantly.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Therefore, the overlap function can be approximated as \begin{equation}O\left(\delta x,\delta p\right) \approx \frac{\mathrm{e}^{ - |\delta \alpha|^2}}{L^2}\left\lvert\sum_{j}^{L-1}\mathrm{e}^{2\mathrm{i}\beta\left( \delta p \cos{\omega_j} - \delta x \sin{\omega_j}\right)}\right\rvert ^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-125: This expression can be further expressed in terms of the first kind of Bessel function $J_n(x)$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:overlap} \begin{aligned} O\left(\delta x,\delta p\right) \approx \frac{\mathrm{e}^{ - |\delta \alpha|^2}}{L^2}\left\lvert\sum_{j}^{L-1}\mathrm{e}^{2\mathrm{i}\beta\left( \delta p \cos{\omega_j} - \delta x \sin{\omega_j}\right)}\right\rvert ^2 = \frac{\mathrm{e}^{ - |\delta \alpha|^2}}{L^2}\left\lvert\sum_{j}^{L-1}\mathrm{e}^{-2\mathrm{i}\beta\sqrt{\delta x^2 +\delta p^2}\sin{(\omega_j + \phi)}}\right\rvert ^2 \approx \frac{\mathrm{e}^{ - |\delta \alpha|^2}}{L^2}\left\lvert \frac{L}{2 \pi} \int_{\phi}^{2\pi+\phi} \mathrm{e}^{-2\mathrm{i}\beta\sqrt{\delta x^2 +\delta p^2}\sin{\theta}} \mathrm{d}\theta \right\rvert ^2 = \mathrm{e}^{ - |\delta \alpha|^2} \left\lvert J_0\left( 2 \beta |\delta \alpha|\right)\right\rvert^2. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The sensitivity of a quantum state to displacements is characterized by the minimal change in displacement, $\delta \alpha$, needed to reduce the overlap between states to zero.

elements.sent-2411-358: These equations are derived on the basis of a series of approximations under the assumption of weak optomechanical coupling, which allows us to consider two thermal baths independently coupled to the optical cavity and the mechanical oscillator.
elements.sent-2411-359: Specifically, we solve the master equation for the time-dependent density matrix $\hat{\rho ^{(\gamma)}$ in the presence of mechanical dissipation at zero temperature and for $\hat{\rho ^{(\kappa)}$ in the case of cavity intensity decay at zero temperature.}}
elements.sent-2411-360: We assume that the initial state at $t=0$ is a direct product of the cavity field state and the mirror state, both of which are coherent states of real-number amplitudes $\alpha_0$ and $\beta_0$; that is, \begin{equation}\hat{\rho}\left(t=0 \right) = \ket{\alpha_0}\bra{\alpha_0}_{\mathrm{c}} \otimes \ket{\beta_0}\bra{\beta_0}_{\mathrm{m}}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-388: The observed decay in the corresponding curves reflects a loss of purity and a reduction in the negative amplitudes of the Wigner function, indicating a gradual degradation of quantum coherence.
elements.sent-2411-389: As a result, key advantages such as enhanced sensitivity to displacements diminish, making the states less effective compared with their fully coherent quantum counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-390: We observe that the enhancement in sensitivity exhibited by our quantum states, as quantified by their overlap functions, disappears in the presence of decoherence.
elements.sent-2411-391: This indicates a substantial loss of sub-Planckian sensitivity, with decoherence having a significant impact.
elements.sent-2411-392: This suggests that the retention of sub-Planckian sensitivity is compromised when decoherence effects become pronounced.
elements.sent-2411-393: This behavior is illustrated in Figs.~\ref{fig:mixed_overlaps and \ref{fig:slice_overlaps , where we set $\alpha_{0 = 8$ and $\beta_{0 = 0$ for a fixed evolution time $t = 2\pi \times 15$, while varying $\gamma$ and $\kappa$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-394: We present the overlap functions under both mild and strong decoherence, using eight-component cat states under unitary evolution as a reference.
elements.sent-2411-395: The results show that the central peak in the oscillatory pattern, which signifies sub-Planckian sensitivity, progressively diminishes, indicating a loss of the enhanced isotropic sensitivity characteristic of the quantum state before decoherence.
elements.sent-2411-396: Importantly, we note that decoherence does not alter the overall shape of the structures, whether isotropic or anisotropic.
elements.sent-2411-397: The original structural patterns are retained but become increasingly blurred as a result of decoherence effects.
elements.sent-2411-398: Note that setting $\gamma = \kappa = 0$ results in the overlap of the eight-component cat state, indicating that our optomechanical analogues exhibit similar enhancement in sensitivity as their counterparts discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:superpositions .}
elements.sent-2411-399: In conclusion, we explore superpositions of $L$ coherent states and investigate their phase spaces using the Wigner function formalism, demonstrating that for $L\ge6$ and even, these superpositions exhibit isotropic sub-Planckian structures in phase space.
elements.sent-2411-400: Previously identified superpositions of compass states~\cite{shukla_superposing_2023 are a particular case of our generalized illustrations, showing that isotropic sub-Planckian structures can also be achieved through superpositions of simple macroscopic cat states.}
elements.sent-2411-401: We proposed an optomechanical framework that generates analogues of the quantum states we suggested, thereby demonstrating the potential for the physical realization of our generic superpositions.
elements.sent-2411-402: We also discussed the decoherence effects associated with the optomechanical setup and noted that the nonclassical characteristics of our cases decay rapidly with larger $\gamma$ and $\kappa$.
elements.sent-2411-403: Real optomechanical systems typically have significantly higher photon frequencies, ranging from $10^3$ to $10^6$ times that of mechanical oscillators~\cite{RevModPhys.86.1391 .}
elements.sent-2411-404: This difference in energy scales raises two key concerns: First, the photon and the mechanical oscillator have very different frequencies, showing that their dynamics are influenced by distinct energy scales.
elements.sent-2411-405: The behavior of the optical cavity is influenced by photons, which have significantly higher energy, whereas optomechanical coupling has a greater effect on mechanical oscillators, which have less energy.
elements.sent-2411-406: Because the time frames and energy transfers differ by orders of magnitude, it is difficult to effectively reproduce the interactions between the two systems.
elements.sent-2411-407: Second, light from an outside source is frequently pumped into the optical cavity to create its modes.
elements.sent-2411-408: The optomechanical coupling constant $k$ is usually much less than the relaxation time of the optical cavity, which is related to the cavity transmission rate.
elements.sent-2411-409: This shows that the relaxation dynamics of the optical cavity affect its interaction with the mechanical oscillator, making it essential to consider these dynamics when one is evaluating the behavior of the system.
elements.sent-2411-410: In a potential experimental realization, the overlap function can be coupled to the system with a two-level atom and read out via the excitation probability of the atom \cite{toscano_sub-planck_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-411: In our analysis, the optomechanical single-photon coupling strength $k$ does not necessarily require strong coupling (referring to the condition where the coupling parameter $k$ is greater than both the mechanical dissipation rate $\gamma$ and the cavity intensity decay rate $\kappa$).
elements.sent-2411-412: However, accurately setting of the coupling parameter $k$ is challenging, and the phenomenon may be disrupted by high-order optomechanical interactions not included in our Hamiltonian [Eq.~(\ref{hami )].}
elements.sent-2411-413: Alternatively, multicomponent cat states have been created by trapped-ion methods~\cite{Li-Xiang,Zheng01031999,PhysRevA.58.761 , which possibly can also be adjusted particularly to generate the cat states we indicated.}
elements.sent-2411-414: For example, in the work reported in Ref.~\cite{Zheng01031999 , a motional superposition of equidistant coherent states arranged on a circle, resulting in multicomponent cat states similar to ours, was achieved through the motion of a trapped ion with the use of a single conditional measurement.}
elements.sent-2411-415: Additionally, such setups have been experimentally realized~\cite{johnson2017ultrafast to generate superpositions of coherent states and perhaps can be further extended to the quantum states we presented.}
elements.sent-2411-416: Importantly, the existence of the sub-Planckian features has important ramifications for the sensitivity of quantum systems to external perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-417: Such sensitivity suggests promising applications in precision measurement and detection, most notably in the detection of weak-forces~\cite{toscano_sub-planck_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-418: In addition to weak force detection, potential applications may include magnetometry with enhanced sensitivity in the measurement of weak magnetic fields, possibly contributing to advancements in medical imaging and fundamental physics experiments, inertial sensing with high-precision accelerometers and gyroscopes based on the principles of sub-Planckian interference, possibly dramatically improving navigation systems, and gravitational wave detection.
elements.sent-2411-419: As the experimental techniques evolve, the integration of sub-Planckian sensitive systems into practical devices could lead to sensors with sensitivity that surpasses classical limits by orders of magnitude.

elements.sent-2411-423: In the main text, we explored the superposition of coherent states with equal amplitudes, uniformly distributed in phase space, with these arrangements providing a circular pattern (for $L\geq4$) around the origin as depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-424: These illustrated cases represent the ideal scenarios for developing isotropic sub-Planckian features, which are directly linked to the isotropic enhancement in sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-425: Now let us consider a more general form of such superpositions, where the component states have unequal spacing and weights: \begin{equation}\label{eq:gener_super} \ket{G_L}\propto\sum_{j=0}^{L-1} A_j\ket{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i} \omega_j}\beta },\end{equation} with relative weights $A_{j}$ and phases $\omega_{j}$.
elements.sent-2411-426: To keep our discussion concise, we focus on a few superpositions of interest, constructed with different weights and/or phases, in order to analyze the effects on the phase-space features resulting from these deviations from the main text.
elements.sent-2411-427: This approach will also provide insights into other related superpositions.
elements.sent-2411-428: First, we consider the case where the coherent states in the superposition (\ref{eq:gener_super ) have equal phases but unequal weights.}
elements.sent-2411-429: We investigate this scenario by examining an eight-component cat state with the arrangement of coherent states shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig17 .}
elements.sent-2411-430: In Fig.~\ref{fig:fig17 , the red and green dots represent the corresponding coherent states, with the green dots indicating the states with relatively smaller weights.}
elements.sent-2411-431: First, consider the case shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig17 (a) and its corresponding Wigner distribution represented in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig18 (a).}}
elements.sent-2411-432: In this case, the coherent states located along the diagonals in phase space are weaker than the other components.
elements.sent-2411-433: As is clearly evident in the inset in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig17 (a), this pattern of the coherent states results in the central feature losing its isotropy, and it closely resembles the central feature of the four-component cat state.}
elements.sent-2411-434: This highlights the significance of the diagonal components in forming the isotropic sub-Planckian structure.
elements.sent-2411-435: Figure~\ref{fig:fig17 (b) and the corresponding Wigner distributions in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig18 (b) represent the case in which the components of the coherent states in the superposition located along the horizontal and vertical axes in the phase space are now weaker than their diagonal components, simply providing the $\pi/4$-rotated case of Fig.~\ref{fig:fig17 (a).}}}
elements.sent-2411-436: It is also apparent that anisotropic sub-Planckian features exist in this case.
elements.sent-2411-437: Now consider the case shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig17 (c), for which the corresponding Wigner distribution is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig18 (c).}}
elements.sent-2411-438: In this case, the components of opposite phases for each pair of coherent states are weakening.
elements.sent-2411-439: Notably, this configuration maintains the isotropy of the sub-Planckian feature.
elements.sent-2411-440: In Fig.~\ref{fig:fig17 (d) and \ref{fig:fig18 (d), the previously uniform distribution of the coherent states is disrupted, leading to the emergence of a highly anisotropic region around the center of the phase space.}}
elements.sent-2411-441: We now examine situations where the phases of the coherent states are random and do not follow the uniform pattern outlined in the main text.
elements.sent-2411-442: We find that deviations from the ideal phase choices, which were initially intended to create isotropic regions in phase space, cause significant disruption to these regions.
elements.sent-2411-443: This is clearly shown in Figs.~\ref{fig:fig20 (a)–\ref{fig:fig20 (d).}}
elements.sent-2411-444: This suggests that in order to create the isotropic sub-Planckian feature, it is essential to construct the superposition by distributing the components equally across the circular region in phase space.
elements.sent-2411-445: The choice of weights plays a crucial role, as the presence of components at opposite phases, even if weaker, ensures the formation of the isotropic features.
elements.sent-2411-446: It is important to note that the sensitivity of states is directly related to the structure of their phase-space features.
elements.sent-2411-447: Specifically, a quantum state with finer phase-space features exhibits a corresponding refinement in sensitivity, as described in Eq.~(\ref{eq:overlap ), and this can be easily understood in the context of each of the cases illustrated.. \twocolumngrid.}
